deck.title: Talksmith
deck.institution: build a talk from what you already know
deck.class: Intro — what it is, how to install it, how to use it

slides.title: Slides are where knowledge goes to die.
slides.sub: The deck-first workflow throws away the expensive part.
slides.notes: Open cold with this line. Pause. Then: 'Who has rebuilt a talk they'd already given, from scratch, because finding the old material was harder than redoing it?' ~2 min.

slides.title: The cost of the blank page
slides.lead: What starting from scratch costs, in three numbers:

stats.caption: typical time to rebuild a class you already gave
of last time's material you actually re-find and reuse
times the same source serves different classes — if you keep it

slides.notes: Numbers are illustrative. The third one sets up the compounding argument. ~2 min.

slides.title: Four problems, one root cause

cards.label: Scattered sources
cards.body: Papers, chats, and downloads live in a dozen places; finding last time's material costs more than redoing it.

cards.label: Knowledge trapped in decks
cards.body: Once it's in slides, you can't query, diff, or reuse it.

cards.label: No reuse across classes
cards.body: Every class starts cold, even when it overlaps last semester's.

cards.label: No record of decisions
cards.body: Why is this slide here? Why was that one cut? The reasoning evaporates.

slides.notes: Four cards, one per pain — each maps to a Talksmith answer you'll show later. ~2 min.

slides.title: This is how you'll work — day to day

image.alt: Talksmith introducing itself in Claude — the workflow chart and a new-vs-resume prompt

facts.label: It's all chat
facts.body: no new UI, no slide editor to learn

facts.label: New or resume
facts.body: start a fresh talk, or pick up one under talks/ where you left off

facts.label: Drop & review
facts.body: add sources, answer a few questions, leave feedback bullets — it does the rest

facts.label: Always in view
facts.body: the Progress and Context panels show what it's reading and doing

slides.notes: Live screenshot from Claude (Cowork). Point out the Progress / Context panels on the right. ~1 min.

slides.title: Not a slide generator

cells: Re-reads the raw pile each query

slides.notes: Land on the last column: same inputs, completely different thing to own afterward. ~3 min.

slides.quote: Instead of re-reading raw documents on every request, compile them once into a persistent, cross-linked knowledge base — the way source code compiles once and runs efficiently thereafter.
slides.notes: Let the quote sit. This is the conceptual license for everything Talksmith does. ~1 min.

slides.title: The three layers

image.alt: Raw sources compiled into the agent-owned wiki, then rendered into disposable projections

facts.label: Raw sources
facts.body: immutable ground truth the agent reads but never rewrites

facts.label: The wiki
facts.body: synthesized, cross-linked Markdown the agent owns and keeps updated

facts.label: Projections
facts.body: final.md, then HTML or .pptx — rendered on demand and disposable

slides.notes: Walk left to right once, then stop and let the diagram breathe. Then: 'I drew that as rough ASCII — Talksmith rendered the SVG.' ~3 min.

slides.title: Your diagrams draw themselves

point.label: You don't draw diagrams
point.body: Talksmith proposes an outline version of each diagram as ASCII right in your draft — edit it if you want — then renders it to a clean, styled SVG at Polish. No more time spent making diagrams look nicer. Every diagram in this talk was generated that way.

slides.notes: The single most surprising feature for newcomers — give it its own beat. ~2 min.

slides.title: Eight steps, driven from chat

facts.body: You talk to Talksmith in chat; it keeps draft.md / final.md as the single source of truth.
The live HTML preview and the final render are optional extras.

slides.notes: This image is the plugin's own workflow diagram — a Markdown image ref, used verbatim. ~3 min.

slides.title: Draft your way

cards.label: Agent Draft
cards.body: It drafts from your corpus, then asks.

cards.label: Presenter Outline
cards.body: You give titles; it fills them.

slides.notes: Most people start with Interview and graduate to Agent Draft once the corpus is rich. ~2 min.

slides.title: Review is a loop
slides.lead: You refine draft.md in rounds — leave inline feedback, it's applied, you review again, until the talk is right.

steps.label: Leave feedback
steps.body: Drop plain-text bullets right in draft.md, under each slide's Presenter feedback — as many as you want, in any editor.

steps.label: Talksmith applies
steps.body: It stamps each bullet, makes the change, and closes it with a one-line resolution kept as an audit trail.

steps.label: Review and repeat
steps.body: Look again, add more feedback, go around once more — the loop runs as many rounds as it takes until you say "final".

image.alt: Feedback lifecycle: raw bullet, stamped open, applied, closed, mirrored to the backlog, then loop

highlights.body: Every round is about the content, not how it looks — visuals and styling come later at Polish.

slides.notes: Meta-moment: open this talk's draft.md and show the real feedback bullets. ~4 min.

slides.title: What you end up with

cards.label: draft.md
cards.body: The working outline you edit, with its feedback audit trail.

cards.label: final.md
cards.body: The polished, delivery-ready derivative — diagrams rendered, feedback stripped.

cards.label: research/corpus/
cards.body: The compiled knowledge base backing every claim.

cards.label: memory.md
cards.body: The progress log that lets you stop and resume anytime.

slides.notes: draft.md survives Polish untouched — final.md is always re-derivable. ~2 min.

slides.title: One source, three outputs
slides.lead: The same final.md renders to any of these — pick per audience:

cards.label: HTML deck
cards.body: a self-contained Reveal.js deck — present in the browser or share a link.

cards.label: PowerPoint
cards.body: a native .pptx (strict or free-form) for Keynote / PowerPoint.

cards.label: PDF
cards.body: export the HTML deck to PDF for handouts.

highlights.body: You're looking at one right now — this deck was rendered from its draft.md with Talksmith.

slides.notes: Tie back to 'slides are a projection': one source, many deliverables — and you're viewing the HTML output live. ~1 min.

slides.title: Five roles, one source of truth

rows.label: Librarian
rows.body: Restructures raw sources into a uniform corpus. Preserves; doesn't compress.

rows.label: Composer
rows.body: Reviews slides against thesis, audience, sources, and learned rules.

rows.label: Editor
rows.body: The muscle: writes draft.md, applies feedback, produces final.md.

rows.label: Illustrator
rows.body: Turns your ASCII into styled SVGs at Polish.

rows.label: Global-Librarian
rows.body: Curates finalized Talks into the shared knowledge library.

slides.notes: You never dispatch these by hand — the orchestrator does, and narrates in plain outcomes. ~3 min.

slides.title: It's Markdown all the way down
slides.lead: Every artifact is a plain .md file — and edits round-trip.

cards.label: Diffable
cards.body: Every change shows up in git diff.

cards.label: Portable
cards.body: The same outline renders to HTML or .pptx.

cards.label: Round-trippable
cards.body: Edited the deck in Keynote? Reconcile it back into draft.md.

image.alt: Forward pipeline draft.md to final.md to pptx, and the reverse pipeline back into draft.md

slides.notes: The round-trip is what makes Markdown-as-source safe: you never lose downstream edits. ~3 min.

highlights.body: The reverse path is optional — you only need it when you've edited the deck outside the cycle (e.g. tweaked the .pptx in PowerPoint).

slides.title: Markdown vs. a binary deck
slides.notes: Binary decks are more WYSIWYG — but that's the projection's job, not the source's. ~2 min.

slides.title: One base, many decks
slides.notes: Four renders of the same talk — the projection point, made visual. ~2 min.

slides.title: Knowledge that compounds
slides.caption: knowledge base per subject that outlives every renderer, every semester, and every teammate handoff.
slides.notes: The hero metric of the whole talk. Say it, then move to close. ~1 min.

slides.title: A semester, compounding

milestones.label: Class 1
milestones.body: Corpus seeded from three papers; first outline drafted.

milestones.label: Class 3
milestones.body: Alice's indexed sources answer Bob's new questions — zero re-reading.

milestones.label: Class 7
milestones.body: Recurring feedback promoted to learnings.md; every future class inherits it.

milestones.label: Next term
milestones.body: A new teammate clones the repo and is productive on day one.

slides.notes: The emotional payoff of 'compounds across classes and co-teachers.' ~2 min.

slides.title: Setup, once
slides.notes: Quick breath before the live demo. ~15 sec.

slides.title: Three commands
slides.code:
# Install the plugin (once per machine), inside a Claude Code session
/plugin marketplace add veigap/talksmith
/plugin install talksmith@talksmith

# Scaffold your subject repo
/talksmith:init

# Open a fresh session in that repo and say:
Hi Talksmith
explanation: /talksmith:init writes exactly one file — a thin CLAUDE.md stub. Commit and push it; teammates who clone the repo are set up automatically.
slides.notes: Do this live if there's a projector. ~4 min.

slides.title: From nothing to running

steps.label: Create
steps.body: one Git repo per subject — the shared home for its material

steps.label: Install
steps.body: the plugin, once per machine, from the marketplace

steps.label: Initialize
steps.body: /talksmith:init drops the CLAUDE.md stub; commit it

steps.label: Start
steps.body: open a session, say "Hi Talksmith", and follow the workflow

slides.notes: Steps 1–3 happen once per repo; step 4 every session. ~2 min.

slides.title: What lives in the repo

rows.label: profile.md
rows.body: subject, audience, duration, language — set once, inherited by every class

rows.label: learnings.md
rows.body: the team's editorial taste, grown from recurring feedback

rows.label: logo.*
rows.body: your institution logo; a neutral placeholder if you skip it

rows.label: talks/
rows.body: one folder per class; where the corpus and outlines accumulate

rows.label: knowledge-library/
rows.body: the curated cross-Talk topic index shared by the team

slides.notes: This folder is where the compounding lives. ~3 min.

slides.title: Quick check
slides.question: You render a .pptx, then tweak it in PowerPoint. What's the source of truth?
slides.explanation: The deck is a projection; edits made downstream are reconciled back into draft.md, so the next Polish re-derives final.md.
slides.notes: Let the room vote by hands before the reveal. The aha: even PowerPoint edits flow back to the Markdown. ~2 min.

slides.title: Key takeaways
panels: Build a knowledge base that compounds, and let the deck be a disposable projection of it.
Three commands to start; chat and draft.md to run; a closed-forever audit trail for every decision.
slides.notes: Mirror the opening hook: the blank page is a choice, and you can stop choosing it. ~2 min.

slides.title: Try it today

items.label: Pick a subject
items.body: Make the one you teach most your first repo.

slides.notes: Leave the install command on screen during Q&A. ~1 min to deliver.

slides.title: Build a talk from what you already know.
slides.notes: Full-bleed closing line. Hold it into Q&A. ~15 sec.
